EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another potent winter storm brought accumulating snow to portions of western Montana.  The hardest hit area was the Potomac/Seeley Lake Region, where snow amounts between 8 to 12 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters between Seeley Lake and Swan Lake reported 8 to 12 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system brought a mixture of snow and freezing rain, accompanied with strong winds to portions of western Montana.  Snow amounts of 7 to 18 inches fell in the valley locations, while 12 to 27 inches fell over the higher terrain.  In addition, sustained winds at 15 to 20 mph were not uncommon with gusts up to 30 mph, causing locally lowered visibility and severe driving conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters in the region reported snow amounts anywhere from 7 to 14 inches.  Winds gusted up to 30 mph creating blowing and drifting snow.  The Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions on area roads, including Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and unstable upper level trough impacted western Montana with moderate to heavy snow fall. In addition, a surge of arctic air was pulled into northwest Montana. This front acted as a focusing mechanism for increased snow production.  Total snow amounts ranged from three to six inches.| |Increased north to east winds produced dangerous winds chills colder than twenty below zero across the northern Flathead valley and Glacier National Park. The combination of winds and snow led to reduced visibility across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination or snow, blowing snow, lowered visibility, and extreme wind chills lead to the declarations of severe driving conditions from Kalispell to Columbia Falls to Coram.  Wind chills dropped down to twenty three below for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system moved into the northern Rockies with abundant moisture.  This system was accompanied by a strong cold front, which pushed through the area over the weekend.  Initially lower elevations started out with a rain snow mix, then transitioned to snow as the the front moved through the area. Snow fall totals in the valley locations ranged between four and eight inches, while the higher terrain received up to a foot of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Deerlodge reported nine inches of new snow accumulations.  Philipsburg reported a foot of new snow accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak weather disturbance moved through northwest Montana late on Thursday night through Friday morning producing widespread precipitation across the region. Although precipitation mainly fell as snow over the higher terrain, a warm layer above the valley floors allowed this precipitation to melt.  Cold air trapped in the Flathead valley allowed for this liquid precipitation to refreeze as it hit the valley floor.  Accumulating ice resulted in numerous school closures due to hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of 0.20 to 0.25 inches built up on surfaces, including roads, in the Flathead valley.  The main location impacted was Kalispell. Numerous area schools were closed due to poor road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and associated cold front moved into the Northern Rockies, creating a favorable environment for strong hail producing thunderstorms and heavy rain.  Flash flooding was reported in the city of Butte with this event as up to an inch of precipitation occurred in 30 to 45 minutes.  Hail up to the size of baseballs and strong winds nearing 70 mph were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved into northwest Montana on the afternoon and evening of the 25th.  Meanwhile, a moist southwest upper level flow moved up and over the arctic airmass, creating accumulating snow and reduced visibility from areas of snow across much of western Montana.  Total snow accumulations of six to eight inches were experienced in many valley locations with snow accumulations over a foot in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters in the area reported widespread wind gusts to 35 mph with heavy snow and blowing and drifting.  Total snow accumulations ranged between four and eight inches, although the blowing and drifting made snow measurments difficult.

Areas of dense fog formed on the morning of February 3rd, lasting into the morning of the 4th across western Montana, including the Flathead and Mission valleys.  Visibility was reduced to one quarter mile or less, causing some accidents to occur on Highway 93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A feed of Pacific moisture ahead of a deep low pressure system brought accumulating snow to elevations above 3500 feet in western Montana.  Locations most impacted included Lookout Pass and Lolo Pass where snow accumulations ranged between eight and twelve inches.  In addition, an entering jet stream produced some gusty winds over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter at Lookout Pass Visitor Center reported ten inches of snow at 5pm on the 24th.  On the morning of the 25th, they had recieved another fourteen inches of snow.  This made a storm total snow of twenty four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of cold Canadian air helped push a cold front south into north central Idaho and southwest Montana. At the same time, moisture from an elongated Pacific weather system streamed up and over this boundary, bringing widespread snow to portions of the Great Divide.  Snow accumulations ranged from two to four inches in the valley locations and up to as much as two feet over the higher terrain.  In addition, strong winds related to the cold front and a strong upper level jet produced strong damaging winds across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in the Columbia Falls area measured winds up to seventy five mph. Numerous trees were reported down across the area, causing fairly widespread power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Eureka reported a camper trailer was blown over due to the winds, which were estimated to reach up to 60 mph.  The Forest Service reported forest blowdowns over hundreds of acres on the Kootenai National Forest and neighboring private lands in the Glen Lake and Indian Creek areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm moved across the Central Plains and Midwest and moved northwest into the Northern High Plains. Wraparound snow and strong winds backed into far Southeastern Montana. Heavy snowfall and strong winds developed on Christmas Day, Friday December 25th, and lasted into Saturday December 26th. Carter and Fallon Counties in the extreme southeast portions of the Billings Forecast Area were hit the hardest with 12-20 inches of snow and 3 to 6 foot drifts. State Highways 7 and 12 were reported closed Friday night and into most of Saturday across these counties. Portions of Custer and Powder River Counties were impacted with snowfall of 6 to 12 inches and difficult traveling conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadus received between 6 and 8 inches of snow along with blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing dry cold front coupled with a strong jet stream aloft allowed for very strong westerly winds, especially over the higher terrain of Glacier National Park. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Logan Pass observation site reported sustained winds between 40 and 56 mph with gusts between 86 to 96 mph for a period of about five hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system brought a mixture of snow and freezing rain, accompanied with strong winds to portions of western Montana.  Snow amounts of 7 to 18 inches fell in the valley locations, while 12 to 27 inches fell over the higher terrain.  In addition, sustained winds at 15 to 20 mph were not uncommon with gusts up to 30 mph, causing locally lowered visibility and severe driving conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 8 to 16 inches were reported by spotters across the region.  Additionally, winds in the forecast zone were sustained anywhere from 13 to 20 mph with gusts up 30 mph.  This created blowing and drifting snow over area roads, causing severe driving conditions on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trough over Western Wyoming combined with a surface low across Colorado resulted in a major early Fall snowstorm across South Central and Southeast Montana. Heavy, wet snow contributed to scattered power outages and numerous automobile accidents. Approximately 3000 homes in the Billings forecast area lost power during the storm due to down tree limbs and power lines. Snowfall amounts ranged from 20-30 inches in the mountains and adjacent foothills with lesser amounts, yet still significant for this time of year, across the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around Melville received 7-8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front came through the region, plummeting temperatures from 20 to 30 degrees below normal.  This had a huge impact on the sugar beet crops in eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area sugar beet producers did not have the crop entirely harvested when a cold front brought very cold air into the region. While the entire crop was finally harvested, this cold snap caused some losses for farmers.   That number has not been quantified yet.  Temperature during this time period ranged from 20 to 30 degrees below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare cut off low pressure system brought severe thunderstorms to southwest Montana.  The main threat with the strong thunderstorms was large hail, measured up to an inch by trained storm spotters.

A rare slow moving upper level low brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of western Montana.  The main threat with these cells was heavy rain and hail.  Hail reached up to an inch in diameter and covered the ground in some locations.  In addition, heavy rain caused flash flooding on the Black Cat burn area, just outside of Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey revealed flood and debris flows near the Black Cat burn area northeast of Frenchtown.  A basement in the area was flooded with two inches of water.  Another residence had water and debris flows running into their backyard and driveway, which briefly cut off access to the home.

Hail reached up to an inch in diameter and covered the ground in some locations.  In addition, heavy rain caused flash flooding on the Black Cat burn area, just outside of Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail three quarter to one inch in diameter accumulated up to two inches deep on the ground. The hail knocked un-cut hay flat and stripped vegetation from shrubs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare slow moving upper level low brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of western Montana.  The main threat with these cells was heavy rain and hail. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Residents in the Quail Ridge subdivision, just south of Lake Blaine Road, endured hail as large as golf balls raining down for up to half an hour, along with very strong winds. Awnings on homes were dented, gardens were flattened, windows broken and basements were flooded from heavy rainfall up to 1 inch with in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm moved across the Central Plains and Midwest and moved northwest into the Northern High Plains. Wraparound snow and strong winds backed into far Southeastern Montana. Heavy snowfall and strong winds developed on Christmas Day, Friday December 25th, and lasted into Saturday December 26th. Carter and Fallon Counties in the extreme southeast portions of the Billings Forecast Area were hit the hardest with 12-20 inches of snow and 3 to 6 foot drifts. State Highways 7 and 12 were reported closed Friday night and into most of Saturday across these counties. Portions of Custer and Powder River Counties were impacted with snowfall of 6 to 12 inches and difficult traveling conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volborg and Ismay received between 8 and 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Canadian system converged with a warm Pacific weather system to produced accumulating snow accross western Montana.  Snow amounts ranged from eight to eighteen inches of new snow.  In addition, as the strong cold front pushed through northwest Montana, northeast winds caused blowing and drifting snow and low visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Columbia Falls reported 9 inches of snow.  A trained spotter 10 miles southwest of Kalispell reported 8 inches of snow.  In addition, strong winds with the passing cold front caused a ground blizzard at Glacier International Airport for a period of time overnight on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the forecast area during the late evening hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th. Upslope flow developed behind the front. This resulted in heavy snow across the foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains with minor accumulations across the plains. However, very slick roads resulted in dangerous traveling conditions. As a result of the icy roads, a 16 year old girl died in a one-vehicle crash on Interstate 90 near Dunmore, Montana. In addition, two women died in a two-vehicle crash on Highway 212, about 8 miles west of Ashland. Although road conditions were icy and snowpacked at the time of the accidents, Montana State Patrol reported speed was also a factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result of the icy roads, a 16 year old girl died in a one-vehicle crash on Interstate 90 near Dunmore, Montana. Although I-90 was icy and snowpacked at the time, Montana Highway Patrol said speed was a factor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought very strong winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glendive AWOS had a peak wind gust of 60 mph at 0035 MST with several hours of winds from 35 to 45 mph sustained.  The Montana Department of Transportation site at the Lindsay Divide had a peak gust of 58 mph at 2322 MST with sustained winds of 35 to 40 mph for several hours.  Weather spotters near Bloomfield reported a peak gust of 58 mph at 0210 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across the forecast area during the late evening hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th. Upslope flow developed behind the front. This resulted in heavy snow across the foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains with minor accumulations across the plains. However, very slick roads resulted in dangerous traveling conditions. As a result of the icy roads, a 16 year old girl died in a one-vehicle crash on Interstate 90 near Dunmore, Montana. In addition, two women died in a two-vehicle crash on Highway 212, about 8 miles west of Ashland. Although road conditions were icy and snowpacked at the time of the accidents, Montana State Patrol reported speed was also a factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two women died in a two-vehicle crash on Highway 212, about 8 miles west of Ashland. Although road conditions were icy and snowpacked at the time of the accidents, Montana State Patrol reported speed was also a factor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought very strong winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 56 mph peak wind gusts around 0100 MST 18 miles south of Wibaux.  Four miles north of that spotter, another one had a gust to 64 mph measured at 0100 MST. Some drifting snow was also reported in the northern portion of the county causing reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system brought a mixture of snow and freezing rain, accompanied with strong winds to portions of western Montana.  Snow amounts of 7 to 18 inches fell in the valley locations, while 12 to 27 inches fell over the higher terrain.  In addition, sustained winds at 15 to 20 mph were not uncommon with gusts up to 30 mph, causing locally lowered visibility and severe driving conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Firehall at Glacier Park International measured a total of 25 inches of new snow by the end of this event.  Other spotters in the region reported anywhere from 12 to 18 inches.  In addition, strong winds sustained at 15 to 20 mph with gusts to 30 mph caused reduced visibility in blowing and drifting snow. The Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions on area roads, with the Flathead County Sheriff declaring emergency travel only through the Kalispell area.  A couple power lines were brought down from the heavy snow, causing power outages in the Whitefish area.  The Kalispell airport was shut down for several hours the morning of January 2nd due to snow on the runway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system brought a mixture of snow and freezing rain, accompanied with strong winds to portions of western Montana.  Snow amounts of 7 to 18 inches fell in the valley locations, while 12 to 27 inches fell over the higher terrain.  In addition, sustained winds at 15 to 20 mph were not uncommon with gusts up to 30 mph, causing locally lowered visibility and severe driving conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters over Lolo pass reported 15 inches of new snow.  Another spotter in the region reported 12 inches of snow in a 24 hour period.  Lolo Pass was closed due to the heavy snow, winds reducing visibility, and the threat of avalanches along Highway 12 crossing into Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific storm system brought a mixture of snow and freezing rain, accompanied with strong winds to portions of western Montana.  Snow amounts of 7 to 18 inches fell in the valley locations, while 12 to 27 inches fell over the higher terrain. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters in the region reported 9 to 12 inches of new snow with weather stations measuring sustained winds up to 20 mph. Blowing and drifting snow caused reduced visibility on area roads, leading the Montana DOT to report severe driving conditions on roads across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary surge of arctic air caused dangerous wind chills along the Continental Divide.  Measured wind chill values ranged between 19 and 26 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northeast winds between 30 and 40 mph ushered a secondary push of arctic air into the area.  Wind chill temperatures ranged between 19 and 26 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front sagged through western Montana creating high winds through the Bitterroot Valley.  Sustained winds up to 60 mph with wind gusts of 70 mph were reported in the valley, with 88 mph winds recorded over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observations between Hamilton and Stevensville recorded wind gusts up to 61 mph.  Storm spotters in the area reported wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph with damage to trees and roofs. A semi-truck was blown over on Highway 93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft brought moisture in the form of heavy snow and rain to portions of western Montana. Liquid precipitation amounts ranged from 1 to 6 inches.  Snow fall amounts ranged between 3 and 13 inches, with the heavier amounts occuring over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained storm spotters in the area reported new snowfall amounts between 8 and 10 inches.  Trees were reported down in the Seeley Lake area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft brought moisture in the form of heavy snow and rain to portions of western Montana. Liquid precipitation amounts ranged from 1 to 6 inches.  Snow fall amounts ranged between 3 and 13 inches, with the heavier amounts occuring over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions along Highway 200 between Greenough and Lincoln, as well as Highway 141 near Avon, and Highway 12 going over MacDonald Pass. The severe conditions were due to heavy snow and blowing snow reducing visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought very strong winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph were reported for several hours on the Glasgow ASOS site.  The peak wind was 72 mph at 2158 MST.  Glasgow 4 miles north had a 75 mph peak gust at 2152 MST, and a site north of Frazer had a 63 mph peak gust at 2201 MST.  The King Coulee RAWS observation had a peak gust of 63 mph on the 2344 MST observation.  While the main impact was the very high winds, the extreme eastern portion of the area had reduced visibilities due to drifting snow as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a winter storm moved through the region. With moisture overrunning this warm front and the snow pack that was on the ground, ice accumulations of over one quarter of an inch happened in parts of the county warning area. Montana Department of Transportation had road reports of severe driving conditions across the region, especially along the major route, US Highway 2 from Wolf Point to the North Dakota state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of one quarter of an inch of ice on the roadways.

This system brought heavy snow to the Absaroka/Beartooth Mountains, as well as the plains of Southern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ashland reported 6-8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system moved across Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming during the afternoon hours of the 26th and continued through the late morning hours of the 27th. This system brought heavy snow to the Absaroka/Beartooth Mountains, as well as the plains of Southern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbus and Nye received anywhere from 7-10 inches of snow with 6 inches reported 1.5 miles northeast of Park City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system, brought in a cold front. Ahead of the front, strong southeast winds occurred. The winds produced blowing and drifting snow. Behind the front, arctic air moved in producing wind chills between 20 below to 30 below zero. Whiteout conditions were caused by sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph across the region. Wind gusts of over 45 mph were reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation Lindsay Divide observation site had several hours of observations with sustained winds or frequent wind gusts over 35 mph. Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches was reported during the event and visibilities were below a quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a winter storm moved through the region. With moisture overrunning this warm front and the snow pack that was on the ground, ice accumulations of over one quarter of an inch happened in parts of the county warning area. Montana Department of Transportation had road reports of severe driving conditions across the region, especially along the major route, US Highway 2 from Wolf Point to the North Dakota state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of more then a quarter of an inch of ice on Interstate 94.  Disaster and Emergency Services reported a jackknifed semi-truck, and the county requested that the Montana Department of Transportation close the interstate.  It remained open but was listed with severe driving conditions on the road reports list.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system, brought in a cold front. Ahead of the front, strong southeast winds occurred. The winds produced blowing and drifting snow. Behind the front, arctic air moved in producing wind chills between 20 below to 30 below zero. Whiteout conditions were caused by sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph across the region. Wind gusts of over 45 mph were reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation Redstone Hill observation site had several hours of observations with wind at or above 35 mph. Also the DOT website reported severe driving conditions on county roads. Whiteout conditions were occurring.  The Sheriff reported ice on Highway 5 between Plentywood and Westby and the road was shut down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought very strong winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation Cow Creek observation site had a peak gust of 63 mph at 2257 MST.  The site had 40 to 50 mph sustained winds for several hours as well.   The Weldon area had a peak gust of 54 mph at 2256 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system, brought in a cold front. Ahead of the front, strong southeast winds occurred. The winds produced blowing and drifting snow. Behind the front, arctic air moved in producing wind chills between 20 below to 30 below zero. Whiteout conditions were caused by sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph across the region. Wind gusts of over 45 mph were reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation observation site at the North Dakota state line had sustained or frequent gusts of 35 mph or more for over three hours. Two inches of snowfall was reported in the area at the same time with visibilities below a quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system, brought in a cold front. Ahead of the front, strong southeast winds occurred. The winds produced blowing and drifting snow. Wind gusts of over 45 mph were reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winnett observation site recorded sustained or frequent gusts of 35 mph or more. Spotters reported visibilities well below a quarter of a mile and two to four inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system, brought in a cold front. Ahead of the front, strong southeast winds occurred. The winds produced blowing and drifting snow. Wind gusts of over 45 mph were reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained and had frequent gusts of 35 mph for several hours. Also two to six inches of snowfall was reported across the county with visibilities well below a quarter mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific storm system, brought in a cold front. Ahead of the front, strong southeast winds occurred. The winds produced blowing and drifting snow. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. The arctic air combined with a moist flow aloft brought a prolonged period of snow. The heaviest snow fell in the mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nye and the Stillwater Mine reported anywhere from 6 to 10 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas in and near Red Lodge reported anywhere from 6 to 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow brought abundant moisture to the Beartooth and Absaroka Mountains. The snow was heavy at times, especially on west facing slopes. In addition, strong winds resulted in blowing and drifting snow creating hazardous conditions and poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Monument Peak picked up 14 inches of snow, while Fisher Creek reported 23 inches of fresh snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major low pressure system moved through the region placing the NWS Glasgow forecast area in the northern end of the system.  Strong east winds of 20 to 30 mph were common with some sites reporting gusts to 45 mph during the event.  The event started in the early morning hours of Sunday March 29th and continued through the late afternoon on Monday March 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to eight inches of snow fell, closing Highways 87 and 244.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major low pressure system moved through the region placing the NWS Glasgow forecast area in the northern end of the system. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 6 inches of snow in Molt, 7 inches in Absarokee, and around 10 inches in Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent band of heavy snow developed over the southern portions of the Billings Forecast Area. This band remained nearly stationary resulting in heavy snowfall, particularly at elevations above 4500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported around 8 inches of snow in Nye.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved into the region starting on January 8, 2009 with freezing rain. An arctic front moved into the area later that day and brought snowfall of up to 8 inches to portions of the region with sustained northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of new snow on the ground. Blowing and drifting snow reduced visibilities to a half mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system moved across Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming during the afternoon hours of the 26th and continued through the late morning hours of the 27th. This system brought heavy snow to the Absaroka/Beartooth Mountains, as well as the plains of Southern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volborg received 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system moved across Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming during the afternoon hours of the 26th and continued through the late morning hours of the 27th. This system brought heavy snow to the Absaroka/Beartooth Mountains, as well as the plains of Southern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas south of Big Timber and Reedpoint(9 to 10 miles south) received around 9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system moved across Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming during the afternoon hours of the 26th and continued through the late morning hours of the 27th. This system brought heavy snow to the Absaroka/Beartooth Mountains, as well as the plains of Southern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fisher Creek, Monument Peak, and White Mill Snotels all received 12-15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A feed of Pacific moisture ahead of a deep low pressure system brought accumulating snow to elevations above 3500 feet in western Montana.  Locations most impacted included Lookout Pass and Lolo Pass where snow accumulations ranged between eight and twelve inches.  In addition, an entering jet stream produced some gusty winds over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Transportation reported 8 inches of new snow accumulations over Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire of an unknown origin consumed 20000 acres of grassland. The blaze  was aided by strong surface winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire of an unknown origin consumed 20000 acres of grassland located near Square Butte. The blaze burned an area 10 miles wide by 5 miles long and was aided by strong surface winds. Approximately 60 firefighters from several communities as well as from federal agencies battled the blaze which took over 8 hours to contain. The fire destroyed one outbuilding and 13 haystacks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and moist winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations over the higher terrain of western Montana.  Amounts ranged between eight to sixteen inches over ski areas and mountain passes.  Lower elevations received anywhere from two to five inches of snow.  In addition, strong south to southwest winds caused blowing and drifting of snow.  Several area roads were closed due to poor driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters near Tuscor and Heron each reported 6 of new snow accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and moist winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations over the higher terrain of western Montana.  Amounts ranged between eight to sixteen inches over ski areas and mountain passes.  Lower elevations received anywhere from two to five inches of snow.  In addition, strong south to southwest winds caused blowing and drifting of snow.  Several area roads were closed due to poor driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters located in Columbia Falls and West Glacier reported generally one to three inches of snow in combination with strong winds reducing visibility to less than one quarter mile.  Higher elevations received anywhere from six to twelve inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and moist winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations over the higher terrain of western Montana.  Amounts ranged between eight to sixteen inches over ski areas and mountain passes.  Lower elevations received anywhere from two to five inches of snow.  In addition, strong south to southwest winds caused blowing and drifting of snow.  Several area roads were closed due to poor driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass reported new snow accumulations of twelve to sixteen inches, while Lost Trail Pass ski area reported fourteen inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous and moist winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow accumulations over the higher terrain of western Montana.  Amounts ranged between eight to sixteen inches over ski areas and mountain passes.  Lower elevations received anywhere from two to five inches of snow.  In addition, strong south to southwest winds caused blowing and drifting of snow.  Several area roads were closed due to poor driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of new snow fell in the lower elevations, with nine to sixteen inches over the higher terrain.  Winds gusting up to 30 mph created blowing and drifting snow with low visibility at times.  Interstate 90 over Lookout Pass was closed due to severe driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and unstable upper level trough impacted western Montana with moderate to heavy snow fall. In addition, a surge of arctic air was pulled into northwest Montana. This front acted as a focusing mechanism for increased snow production.  Total snow amounts ranged from three to six inches.| |Increased north to east winds produced dangerous winds chills colder than twenty below zero across the northern Flathead valley and Glacier National Park. The combination of winds and snow led to reduced visibility across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination or snow, blowing snow, lowered visibility, and extreme wind chills lead to the declarations of severe driving conditions along US Highway 2.  Wind chills dropped down to thirty one below for fourteen hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second major snowstorm and blizzard within a weeks time brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. This storm impacted areas that were hit hard by the March 23-24 storm. An upper level low pressure moved out of the Pacific Northwest and moved across Idaho and Wyoming. At the same time, a strong surface low pressure developed over Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. Winds across the area were sustained in the 25 to 35 mph range with gusts from 30 to 40 mph. These winds combined with heavy snow resulted in visibilities being reduced to a quarter mile at many locations. In addition, snowfall exceeded 12 in Carbon, Stillwater and Custer Counties. The storm resulted in one death. A 19-year-old woman was killed on Highway 39 near Forsyth after losing control of her car on the snow covered highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyola received 9 inches of snow while Lodge Grass received 12 inches. Both cities experienced blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought up to 12 inches of snow to a small part of northeast Montana. The best accumulations occurred in the highest elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow late in the morning over the western portion of the county and in the afternoon over the east. Most portions of the county received about 8 inches, especially in higher terrain locations. Only a half inch or less occurred near Fort Peck Lake (Hell Creek Marina).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought up to 12 inches of snow to a small part of northeast Montana. The best accumulations occurred in the highest elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated to 6 inches accompanied by blowing and drifting. Visibility was reduced to half a mile as a result.  The rural electric cooperative reported damage due to icing that occurred on the lines, and some power poles and insulators.  This was mostly in the Peerless, Lustre, Larslan, Opheim and Glentana areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought heavy wet snow to portions of North Central Montana. Snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Stanford. In addition, 9 inches of snow fell at Martinsdale 13N (the Daisey Peak SNOTEL site - elevation 7600 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought heavy wet snow to portions of North Central Montana. Snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nineteen inches of snow fell at Lewistown 18SSW (the Crystal Lake SNOTEL site - elevation 6050 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought heavy wet snow to portions of North Central Montana. Snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Montana City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought heavy wet snow to portions of North Central Montana. Snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen inches of snow fell at White Sulphur Springs 17W (the Pickfoot Creek SNOTEL site - elevation 6650 feet). Seven inches of snow fell at White Sulphur Springs 40N (the Onion Park SNOTEL site - elevation 7410 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late spring Pacific storm brought blizzard conditions to portions of North Central Montana. Snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches was reported along with gusty winds and visibilities less than one-quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen inches of snow fell at Carter 18N. In addition to the snow, winds gusting to 40 mph occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across the area during the evening of the 25th. This front combined with a surface area of low pressure that moved across Northeast Wyoming on the 26th resulted in heavy snow across portions of Southeast and South Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fisher Creek Snotel reported 9 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty inches of snow fell at Carter 27NW.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miles City and Volborg reported 12 inches of snow along with blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally warm temperatures caused increased snow melt over much of western Montana, with many rivers and small streams running at or slightly above normal. Some minor damage was reported along Fifer Creek in southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Basement flooding was reported by the DES Coordinator in two homes along Fifer Creek on Hemlock Street in Anaconda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second major snowstorm and blizzard within a weeks time brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. This storm impacted areas that were hit hard by the March 23-24 storm. An upper level low pressure moved out of the Pacific Northwest and moved across Idaho and Wyoming. At the same time, a strong surface low pressure developed over Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. Winds across the area were sustained in the 25 to 35 mph range with gusts from 30 to 40 mph. These winds combined with heavy snow resulted in visibilities being reduced to a quarter mile at many locations. In addition, snowfall exceeded 12 in Carbon, Stillwater and Custer Counties. The storm resulted in one death. A 19-year-old woman was killed on Highway 39 near Forsyth after losing control of her car on the snow covered highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Billings and surrounding area received between 6 and 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous storm system brought snow to portions of western Montana. Snow levels remained above valley elevations initially, but the lower elevations of northwest Montana saw light accumulations. Local gusty west to southwest winds increased as the jet stream pushed through the area, creating areas of reduced visibility in blowing snow.  Snow accumulations in the lower elevations ranged from three to five inches, while higher terrain received eight to twelve inches of new snow. Wind gusts between thirty and forty miles per hour were not uncommon with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters in Anaconda and around Georgetown Lake reported eight to twelve inches of new snow accumulations over at twelve hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved across Wyoming bringing heavy wet snow and strong winds to Southeast Montana on the 22nd and 23rd. Whiteout conditions due to very strong northerly winds were common. U.S. Highway 212 from Broadus to Ekalaka to the Wyoming State line was closed, as well as all other county roads. Drifts up to 8 feet high were reported across the county. Motorists were stranded across Powder River and Carter Counties. Five semitrailers got stuck south of Hammond which hindered Montana plows. In an attempt to rescue stranded motorists, two Department of Transportation trucks became stuck and stranded between Boyce and Hammond in Carter County. The stranded motorists and DOT drivers spent the night in the cab of one of the DOT trucks before making it back to safety the next day. In addition, a woman and her baby had to spend the night in a Suburban 17 miles west of Alzada on Highway 212. Three people were also rescued on Route 323 between Ekalaka and Alzada. Elsewhere across Southern Montana, heavy snow fell but amounts were much less, and strong winds were not a factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Birney and Ashland each received 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought cold temperatures, snowfall and very strong winds to the region. Blizzard conditions were reported in five counties of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lindsay Divide Department of Transportation site had winds from the northwest at 35 to 45 mph sustained with gusts up to 53 mph for several hours.  Combined with the snow on the ground, visibilities were reduced to less than a quarter mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A localized wind gust occurred at Duck Creek near Fort Peck Lake. Most of the thunderstorms brought localized heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in 10 minutes amounted to 0.30. Total rainfall 0.88 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system moved across the forecast area on the 23rd and 24th. Precipitation began as rain during the afternoon of the 23rd and changed to snow during the overnight hours. The snow was heavy at times especially over the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melville received around 10-11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough caused some multicellular thundershowers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak thunderstorm collapsed over Sidney.  The only damage reported was an old roof was blown off of an old building near the sugar plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multicellular thunderstorms were in the area.  One produced a quick severe gust.  No damages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm cell near Glendive collapsed and produced a single gust of 58 mph.  No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing surface low pressure over eastern Montana helped to pull a warm and unstable airmass into the region from the south by the afternoon. An upper trough moving toward the area from the Pacific Northwest provided an extra contributor to the development of the thunderstorms. The thunderstorms were only observed to be severe within a small area in Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A traveler reported quarter-size hail on Highway 2 from one mile west to four miles west of Frazer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass led to a widespread outbreak of severe weather during the afternoon hours. Hail the size of golf balls and wind gusts in excess of 60 mph was reported. Power outages occurred in many cities as numerous trees and power lines were blown down by storm winds. Heavy rain was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 61 mph at White Sulphur Springs 40NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of the area experienced a drought, reaching a D2 status on the US Drought Monitor.   Reduced crop yields for hay and wheat were the main impact of this drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While mountain snow melt helped bring up Fort Peck Lake almost 20 feet from a year prior, and provide good flows on the Milk River, below normal precipitation for the months of May and June led to some areas of Valley County to experience drought conditions.  Hay yields were significantly reduced and wheat was sparse and not as tall as it should be for this time of year.  June was the fourth driest on record for Glasgow.  The worst of the drought was along the Milk River and about 25 miles north and south of the Milk River.   When precipitation did fall, it was often quickly evaporated by strong and gusty winds.  The sub-soil moisture was not very good either according to area farmers.  The drought continued into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Cascade Fire Area of 2008 caused the West Fork of Rock Creek to rise out of its banks. Two debris flows occurred on the south side of the West Fork of Rock Creek covering West Fork Road. In addition, two debris flows occurred on the north side of the West Fork of Rock Creek. The water level on the West Fork of Rock Creek was greater than 4 feet above normal and flowing outside of its banks at the peak of the event. Data shows that 1.85 inches of rain fell within three hours over the burn area with additional rainfall occuring beyond three hours. As a result of the debris flows and landslides, large boulders and downed trees covered West Fork Road.

The water level on the West Fork of Rock Creek was greater than 4 feet above normal and flowing outside of its banks at the peak of the event. Data shows that 1.85 inches of rain fell within three hours over the burn area with additional rainfall occuring beyond three hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt caused streams within Carter County to run high. In addition to Box Elder Creek, minor flooding was also reported along the Little Missouri River and other streams. Residents along Box Elder Creek from near Hammond to Ridgeway, Belltower and Mill Iron also experienced minor flooding. In addition, heavy rain showers resulted in additional rises to the streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt caused streams within Carter County to run high. In addition to Box Elder Creek, minor flooding was also reported along the Little Missouri River and other streams. Residents along Box Elder Creek from near Hammond to Ridgeway, Belltower and Mill Iron also experienced minor flooding. In addition, heavy rain showers resulted in additional rises to the streams. One rancher reported fence repairs totally $80,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved across Wyoming bringing heavy wet snow and strong winds to Southeast Montana on the 22nd and 23rd. Whiteout conditions due to very strong northerly winds were common. U.S. Highway 212 from Broadus to Ekalaka to the Wyoming State line was closed, as well as all other county roads. Drifts up to 8 feet high were reported across the county. Motorists were stranded across Powder River and Carter Counties. Five semitrailers got stuck south of Hammond which hindered Montana plows. In an attempt to rescue stranded motorists, two Department of Transportation trucks became stuck and stranded between Boyce and Hammond in Carter County. The stranded motorists and DOT drivers spent the night in the cab of one of the DOT trucks before making it back to safety the next day. In addition, a woman and her baby had to spend the night in a Suburban 17 miles west of Alzada on Highway 212. Three people were also rescued on Route 323 between Ekalaka and Alzada. Elsewhere across Southern Montana, heavy snow fell but amounts were much less, and strong winds were not a factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Albion received 24 inches of snow with Alzada receiving 15 inches. Strong winds created blizzard conditions, along with 6 to 8 foot drifts. Please see Winter Storm/Blizzard March 29-30 for more details and damage amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved across Wyoming bringing heavy wet snow and strong winds to Southeast Montana on the 22nd and 23rd. Whiteout conditions due to very strong northerly winds were common. U.S. Highway 212 from Broadus to Ekalaka to the Wyoming State line was closed, as well as all other county roads. Drifts up to 8 feet high were reported across the county. Motorists were stranded across Powder River and Carter Counties. Five semitrailers got stuck south of Hammond which hindered Montana plows. In an attempt to rescue stranded motorists, two Department of Transportation trucks became stuck and stranded between Boyce and Hammond in Carter County. The stranded motorists and DOT drivers spent the night in the cab of one of the DOT trucks before making it back to safety the next day. In addition, a woman and her baby had to spend the night in a Suburban 17 miles west of Alzada on Highway 212. Three people were also rescued on Route 323 between Ekalaka and Alzada. Elsewhere across Southern Montana, heavy snow fell but amounts were much less, and strong winds were not a factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadus and surrounding areas reported 3-4 foot drifts resulting in all roads being closed. Numerous vehicles along with a couple state plows were stranded. Carter and Powder River Counties lost more than 4,000 head of livestock, mostly newborn calves, during the three back-to-back blizzards during March and April. Dollar amounts from losses were not available from Powder River County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second major snowstorm and blizzard within a weeks time brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. This storm impacted areas that were hit hard by the March 23-24 storm. An upper level low pressure moved out of the Pacific Northwest and moved across Idaho and Wyoming. At the same time, a strong surface low pressure developed over Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. Winds across the area were sustained in the 25 to 35 mph range with gusts from 30 to 40 mph. These winds combined with heavy snow resulted in visibilities being reduced to a quarter mile at many locations. In addition, snowfall exceeded 12 in Carbon, Stillwater and Custer Counties. The storm resulted in one death. A 19-year-old woman was killed on Highway 39 near Forsyth after losing control of her car on the snow covered highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ekalaka received 8-10 inches of snow along with blizzard conditions. Many livestock and new born calves were buried in the snow. Records show $1,049,659 in losses for livestock in Carter County. This figure does not include what would be an average death loss during calving season, this was above and beyond the usual. This number is from about 140 livestock producers in the county. About 60 more producers probably had losses, but did not report or may not have hit the threshold for reporting. The average for producers reporting was about $7500 of loss. It was very stressful for ranchers with back-to-back storms with snow so deep there was no place left to put livestock. However, there were no serious power outages and no lives lost or serious injuries to residents. |In addition, road damage was severe with the county losing two bridges due to flood damage as the snow rapidly melted due to a quick warm-up after the storm. The flooding also did a lot of damage to fences as one rancher reported $80,000 worth of damage to fences alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trough over Western Wyoming combined with a surface low across Colorado resulted in a major early Fall snowstorm across South Central and Southeast Montana. Heavy, wet snow contributed to scattered power outages and numerous automobile accidents. Approximately 3000 homes in the Billings forecast area lost power during the storm due to down tree limbs and power lines. Snowfall amounts ranged from 20-30 inches in the mountains and adjacent foothills with lesser amounts, yet still significant for this time of year, across the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Fork Shields Snotel received between 8 and 15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front came through the region, plummeting temperatures from 20 to 30 degrees below normal.  This had a huge impact on the sugar beet crops in eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area sugar beet producers did not have the crop entirely harvested when a cold front brought very cold air into the region. While the entire crop was finally harvested, this cold snap caused some losses for farmers.   That number has not been quantified yet.  Temperature during this time period ranged from 20 to 30 degrees below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients across Southern Montana resulted in high winds at the Yellowtail Dam in Southern Big Horn County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 78 mph was reported at the Yellowtail Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream over the state combined with surface low pressure over Southern Canada created tight pressure gradients across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area. As a result...high winds were observed at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph were recorded in Harlowton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system moving through the Great Basin produced strong winds across much of western Montana. Winds peaked between 50-60 mph, causing damage to trees and power lines, particularly across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported downed trees across area roads, power outages, and phone outages due to trees down on power lines.  The main impact was to areas from Kalispell, to Whitefish, to Polson and Bigfork.  Eleven boats were damaged around Flathead Lake with dock damaged at Lakeside and Finley Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare slow moving upper level low brought a round of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of western Montana.  The main threat with these cells was heavy rain and hail.  Hail reached up to an inch in diameter and covered the ground in some locations.  In addition, heavy rain caused flash flooding on the Black Cat burn area, just outside of Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter two miles north of Creston reported wind gusts up to 60 mph.  In addition small hail was reported with this storm. The Creston Fire Department reported|extensive damage from numerous trees blown down onto power lines. A sailboat was tipped over on Blaine Lake with four people thrown into the water. A large fir tree was blown down onto a home on Bachelor Grade Road. Up to 100 trees were blown over along Bachelor Grade Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous late autumn storm brought heavy snow and cold temperatures to a portion of North central Montana. Snow amounts exceeded 20 inches at some mountain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirty-two inches of snow fell at White Sulphur Springs 40N (the Onion Park SNOTEL site - elevation 7400 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across the Pacific Northwest brought moisture into Montana from the south while cold air was moving into the state from Canada. The result was winter storm conditions, mainly confined to elevations above 5000 feet. Snow amounts in excess of 6 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Lewistown 25S (elevation 5575 feet).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive hail damage to homes, roofs, and vehicles reported on Green Acres and Continental Drive near Butte.  Hail size ranged between 1.75 and two inches in diameter.  In addition, wind gusts were estimated up to 70 mph, which caused some tree branches to break.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and associated cold front moved into the Northern Rockies, creating a favorable environment for strong hail producing thunderstorms and heavy rain.  Flash flooding was reported in the city of Butte with this event as up to an inch of precipitation occurred in 30 to 45 minutes.  Hail up to the size of baseballs and strong winds nearing 70 mph were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on both Elm and Silverbow Street.  A few homes on Silverbow had flooding in their basements.  The Butte Plaza Mall parking lot was flooded, and strong winds caused water to flow into the doorways and walkways of the mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low tracked south from the Pacific Northwest into Utah and then Wyoming. Circulation from this storm brought moisture into Montana where it overran cold air at the surface. Snowfall of greater than 6 inches was reported at elevations above 6000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at Dupuyer 23WSW (the Badger Pass SNOTEL site - elevation 6900 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low tracked south from the Pacific Northwest into Utah and then Wyoming. Circulation from this storm brought moisture into Montana where it overran cold air at the surface. Snowfall of greater than 6 inches was reported at elevations above 6000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual upper low moved northeast from off the California coast, bringing thunderstorms with heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall measured between 730 pm and 930 pm was 4.66 inches. 2.5 inches fell in the first 15 minutes with pea-sized hail.  Runoff from water reached near record high water markers near the bridge.  Water was about 18 inches below the bridge span.  No significant damage was reported and the flooding occurring in a very rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual upper low moved northeast from off the California coast, bringing thunderstorms with heavy rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain gauge that can hold up to six inches had overflowed.  Bear Gulch was about two to three feet out of its banks.  Beaver Creek was at least four feet out of its banks in the area.  The water went over a fence on a coulee three-quarter miles north of Beaver Creek.  This is a very rural area and no damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across areas mostly south of the Missouri River. The greatest damage occurred in Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bow echo thunderstorm raked through the area destroying sheds, wiping out crops and hay fields, and causing significant loss of property.  The bow echo started producing severe wind gusts and at least 1/2 hail about 25 miles northwest of Jordan in the Brussett area and tracked east-northeast through the county.   Golf ball sized hail was reported with the winds south of the Hell Creek area.  Twenty-six families were impacted with damage to their structures, livestock and crops.   A total of 213,440 acres of rangeland, wheat and hay fields were damaged partially to totally wiped out.  Six horses and a cow had to be put down due to injuries they received during the storm.  Damage information was gathered from the Jordan Tribune, and the USDA FSA agent in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved east across areas mostly south of the Missouri River. The greatest damage occurred in Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated wind gusts in excess of at least 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind moved and lifted wooden pallets.  The closest reporting station is about 2 miles away and the winds at the time were gusting to 40 mph for a three hour time span.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wooden pallets moved and thrown around downstream from origination point. Occurred 4 W of Fort Peck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in northeast Montana created significant rainfall especially in Richland County. Areas of large hail and strong winds occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall of 3.08 inches, 2.72 fell through midnight.  Water flowed over local roadway, washing some gravel off, but no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in northeast Montana created significant rainfall especially in Richland County. Areas of large hail and strong winds occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some fields had some wash outs in them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light south to southeast wind brought reports of up to eight inches of light fluffy snow to a small area on the north side of the reservoir.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An small area of lake affect snow occurred early in the morning, mainly before dawn, from the dam, north to Park Grove and west to the town of Fort Peck.  The highest report was eight inches just west of the Dredge Cuts.  Five inches were reported in the town of Fort Peck. The Duck Creek Road (where it intersects Highway 24) was about where the accumulating snow began/ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong supercells formed over the region, producing large hail, damaging winds, funnel clouds and at least one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter received 1.1 inches of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 58 mph at the Great Falls ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and associated cold front moved into the Northern Rockies, creating a favorable environment for strong hail producing thunderstorms and heavy rain. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny size hail was reported in Busby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across the central portions of the Billings County Warning Area during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was estimated 2 miles east of Hysham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell thunderstorm moved southeast through the area.
Strong supercells formed over the region, producing large hail, damaging winds, funnel clouds and at least one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny-sized hail was reported. Winds were estimated at around 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms erupted across the area for an unusual late-season event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell for about five minutes with only 0.30 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance that was moving from southwest to northeast, brought severe thunderstorms to northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of dissipating storms moved east through the area between 1130 pm and 1230 pm. The outflow caused a microburst. The homeowner reported that the winds hit her home at about 1200 and 1205 am. She was not injured.|A new modular home was slightly lifted and pushed back off its foundation.  The house dropping back on the foundation and it caused the I-beams below the house to bend. The house was sitting on a very sturdy foundation, but it did not have any strapping to hold it to the foundation. There were two outbuildings significantly damaged as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance that was moving from southwest to northeast, brought severe thunderstorms to northeast Montana.
